checks.a_analysis: I have compared each heading, paragraph, and table row in the output JSON with the provided source images and OCR text. All text in the output corresponds directly to content found in the source document. No fabricated sections, headings (like 'Page 1 Content'), or invented body text were found.

The source document does not contain any contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses, or URLs). I have reviewed the output JSON and verified that no such information has been fabricated or erroneously added.

The source document consists of three pages. Page 1 (2024 Data Collection Overview), Page 2 (2025 Data Collection Map), and Page 3 (Collaboratory details) are all represented in the output JSON. Section 1 covers the contents of Page 1, Section 2 covers Page 2, and Section 3 covers Page 3. No pages or major sections were omitted.

The legends on Page 1 and Page 2/3 are rendered as tables in the JSON. The legend on Page 1 contains 5 rows corresponding to the 5 phases of LiDAR data collection, which matches the JSON. The legend on Page 2 contains 4 entries, and the JSON contains a table with 4 rows (1 header and 3 data rows). The 'QL = Quality Level' box is also captured as a single-row table. No data rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I examined the text flow in each section. While some text labels integrated into the graphics (specifically the map title 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina' and the callout boxes on the Page 1 map) were not extracted as separate text elements, they are described in the image alt-text. The core informative text—including the headers, paragraphs, legends, and the detailed list on Page 3—is fully present. The omissions do not exceed the 25% threshold for any section.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: I performed a word-for-word comparison of the text in the JSON against the source document. The dates (e.g., May 2025, 1/11-1/12), technical terms (e.g., NAD83(2011), QL 1, DEM), and geographical names (e.g., Alexander) are all transcribed accurately. There are no meaning-altering changes or errors in the text content.

checks.a_analysis: The output document correctly represents the content of the three-page source PDF. The title '2025 Data Collection Area' and the map of Western North Carolina are repeated on pages 2 and 3 of the source document, and the output document reflects this by including them in their respective sections (sections 1 and 2). There is no unintended duplication of text blocks or paragraphs.

The heading hierarchy is consistent. Each major page title is correctly identified as a level 1 heading (H1). On page 3, '2025 Data Collection Area' and 'North Carolina Collaboratory' are both rendered as Level 1 headings; while the latter is visually a sub-header, maintaining a flat Level 1 structure for main titles across pages is acceptable and no heading levels are skipped (e.g., H1 to H3).

The document contains several visual elements represented as tables (the map legends and the 'Quality Level' definition box). The structure of these tables accurately reflects the source. For example, the 'Phase & Quality' legend on page 1 is correctly mapped as a table with a header row and five data rows. The '2025 Data Collection Area' legend on page 2 is also correctly mapped.

The reading order follows a logical flow that matches the visual layout of the PDF. On page 1, the document reads from the header to the legend, then the text boxes, and finally the map. On page 3, the bulleted list (which is to the right of the map) is placed before the map in the reading order, which is a standard accessibility practice for prioritizing text content over graphics.
The bulleted list on page 3 is correctly identified and converted into an unordered list with eight distinct items, matching the eight bullets in the source document. No items are merged or missing.
There are no links in the source document, and none have been incorrectly injected into the output.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the converted HTML document faithfully follows the flow of the source PDF. Content from each page (titles, maps, legends, text boxes, diagrams, and logos) is presented in a logical linear order. Map legends have been extracted into accessible tables while the maps themselves remain present as images. There are no multi-column collapses or rearrangements that confuse the meaning of the document.

The tables in the HTML document, which correspond to map legends and key text boxes in the PDF, are correctly rendered. They have clear borders, distinct headers, and aligned columns, making the data easily readable and understandable for a sighted user.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes both maps, the LiDAR diagram, and the various agency logos. No broken image icons or missing meaningful graphics were observed.

The map 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina' and its associated caption are visually duplicated at the end of the document. While the source PDF contains this map on both pages 2 and 3, the HTML output renders it three times: once for page 2, once for page 3 (within the Collaboratory section), and a third time at the very end of the document after the logos that conclude the page 3 content.
checks.b_evidence_source: The 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina' map appears once on Page 2 and once on Page 3.
checks.b_evidence_output: The 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina' map and caption are rendered three times total. The final instance appears at the very bottom of the document, immediately following the DPS and EMAP logos.
checks.b_evidence_location: Bottom of the HTML document, following the 'North Carolina Collaboratory' section content.
